Totally wrap this thing up from just a uh a web management system that works with public data only.

I need to think about that,
right?
Is that fair?
That is fair.
Um, and so I have thought about that and it actually makes sense when you think about, you know, if you look at
What the advertised price was for a unit when it was leased.

That might be more relevant than what was actually booked in Yardi or whatever the PMS is because, you know, it could have been a, you know, a shorter lease term that had some sort of premium on it or, you know, they could have had some sort of looking at lease special.
Um, and if that's the case, we can, we can kind of dig our way in and find out about it, but it, it almost in some ways it's almost more relevant.
To see what the public facing price was when the lease was executed.
I don't know if you guys have thought about it from that perspective.
Yeah, it makes sense.

I mean, we just have generally found that it's especially if you're if you're laying on the concessions we're seeing and getting from there to a net effective rent, um, it just tends to be pretty dang close uh to what the actual leases signed are, and, um, certainly it's the perspective that other renters are taking as well.
I think ultimately what we've found is since we last talked, we've done a fair amount of work on the data science side of this product, so building out a robust demand model.
It's a machine learning model that's trained off of all the data that we've got in apartment IQ and then a price elasticity model as well.

So it can tell, you know, basically these two models together can tell us at your current price point how many leases are you going to sign, and depending on how much you change it, how will that impact how many leases you'll sign.
Um, what we found is When people compare the system that's based off of public data only versus a system that's based on the data they have internally, it's just pretty close already.
Um, and so some of these things, you know, that, that you might not see uh without a red roll.
Don't end up mattering as much.

It's and um the flip side though is without a rent roll, we really can't see more than, you know, if you're sending 90 day renewal notices and posting availability like if you get a notice that they're not renewing and that goes to your website 60 days in advance.
We're just not going to be able to, we can't tell you what's likely to be exposed 6 months from now because we, we're not seeing your rent roll, we're just seeing what's available now.
So renewals becomes more of a, a question mark because that's so much of that is just seeing your actual rent roll and the expiration curve from that.
Sure, that makes sense.
I can't remember how do you all manage renewals today?
So, uh, we use AIRM as our revenue management tool and so it has, you know, full access to everything.
So we, we manage it through, through, uh, our revenue management tool.
OK.
Got it.
And, and have you turned off the private, uh, non-public data in A or is it still pumping through?

I don't know if you heard about that, uh, but yeah, they, they just, and, and they've, they've gone to and it was interesting because I was gonna try to hook you guys up with someone from RealPage cause I used to work for them.

I still know a lot of people there cause I wanted your feed to go into the market feed that AIRM uses, but they, RealPage announced that they're using a publicly, public data screen scraping feed to feed there.
Market, uh, their market price tool in AIRM, so I guess they kind of, they realized that was the way to go.

In general, yeah,
you,

you could say the same thing about Yardi there too at the same time, uh, so, yeah, yeah, I got, I, I'm trying to get Yardi to allow us to push concession data into, into their system and I, I kind of, I kind of gave up on that.
I don't think that's gonna happen.

and we can kind of go from there if that works for you.

Uh, the first thing that that we should say is we've, we've built out the settings of this a bit more robustly, and so you can you can uh optimize for occupancy in a property or a bed group level.
Um, and so most of our customers now are doing this in a bed group level basis.
I can't remember if that's something you and I talked about at all.

Um, there's additional settings, being able to set daily and weekly price limit changes, you know, often folks want to put like, you know, like a 3% max daily change on, uh, uh, price 7% max weekly, sometimes folks change that.
We've also got a setting that enables you to pause recommendations after a price change.
Uh, for 24 hours just enables you to get a chance to see, OK, did the price change that I input actually make a difference.
So, so in general, these properties that I'm gonna show you today are optimizing for uh bed count level pricing
uh and occupancy and so you'll see some changes here.
What the system is doing that you're, you're probably gonna see here is it's optimizing for in this case, we have 95% as the goal across the board for each of these bed counts.
And uh to remind you how this is working, it's looking at the current occupancy of this bed group.

And then it's projecting based on this current price point, how many leases we think it's gonna that that property is going to get on a 56 day time frame is the forecast time frame that we're working off
of.
OK.
And you can see some other things, exposure for that property, etc.
um, but basically what it's saying is, listen, if your current price point is headed towards a number that's not your sustainable budget occupancy, etc.
we're gonna recommend the price that's gonna get you to it.
And you can see that's what's happening here.
So in this case on the studios, you know, they're, they've got uh we're projecting a 3-lea shortfall.
Because we're only projecting 2 leases uh in the next 56 days and they need 5.
Now the demand model that's working here.

It is unique to us About 40% of the demand model comes from your property's data, so the historicals for these studios, and about 60% is coming from the market that surrounds those, and that's just um been the most predictive combination.
And so it is looking not only just at your historicals and your price, but we found that the models are significantly more predictive if you're also factoring in your comps.

So, To drill a layer down and start to get a more understanding of what's going on here, you can drill in and what this is meant to do is really start to combine the data you can get from apartment IQ directly to your revenue management tool.

And so this would give you all the, it's pulling comps from your market survey currently, but you can edit that if you'd like, and it gives you a sense of whose movie prices, how, what's the overall market doing.
Um, on a time frame and just like an apartment IQ you can change this for any time period you'd like.
You can look at available units, you can look at leased units, see if those are different, and you can also drill down into a floor plan level.
So that you could separate in this case, they've got S ones but they don't have S2s.
And so this really is meant to, from what we heard from a lot of our customers, and maybe this resonates with you.

You're in your revenue management tool all day and then when you find a recommendation, you're not so sure about that's when apartment IQ gets put out to find out like how do I, you know, how do I understand how this flows with the market.

And the other component of using a tool like AM is, at least historically, they really haven't even told you who your comps are and given you a lot of granularity or transparency into what's going on there.
And so this is an effort to create more transparency in the system and give you that sense.

Yeah, we have to, I mean, I, I was telling uh Matt and Phil our last call, on my pricing calls I have Arum and apartment IQ up running, and I bounced back and forth between them because I need to see, you know, where we are priced versus our apartment IQ comps.
Yeah, so getting this into one screen would be helpful.
Yep, great.
And so this is combining those, it also, you'll probably recognize this from Harvard IQ, but uh these are the historicals as well, so you can see what was happening last year for my property.

Uh, you can also see what was happening at competitive properties this year, last year, or this time last year, you can look at, or you can go much further back if you'd like, uh, all the options that you're used to seeing, um.

We've recently added concession values in here as well, so you can see on a specific basis who's offering concessions, when, and you can look at their value versus a percentage of rent to understand how that's impacting the market, uh, as well.

And then of course you can go back and see, OK, somebody really reducing their prices because they've just got a ton of exposure and they need to buy occupancy, or is that actually a trend that I should take a look at?

Um, more closely and each of these charts, you probably already know this, but each of these charts now have a drill down that enables you to see, so we're looking on a daily basis now, but if I was to take this back and say, OK, I'd like to see this on a monthly basis in the last 6 months, it now enables you to see, OK, who's signing leases?
What are they?
And um, well it looks like they're still building that now, but uh you'll be able to see the exact leases that those are.

So combining that market data with your revenue management tool, um, and then as you'd expect, continue to drill down into what am I leasing, um, one of the data points that um We're looking at is obviously your average lease rent, which I'm sure you're used to from A and this is being calculated by the average of your previous 5 leases um that you've signed as long as those 5 leases are within a 90 day period.
So you're able to drill in, see that as well.
You can see your exposure, uh, what units those are, etc.
etc.
and this is completely operating uh off of data that we've collected from the web.

on the web, so can you see our actual availability?

Is that,
yeah, that's how you look at it,
yeah, yeah, I, I don't, I don't want anyone else's rent roll data.

OK, we are doing integrations with the PMSs right now.

Um, so in Q2, there will be a version of this that combines your private rent and roll data with the public market data, and that will enable you to do everything that you would be used to want to do, managing renewals, seeing out, you know, uh, projecting expiration, curves, etc.
That's 12, most of the time we can get around, um, usually folks will just set like a cap on the number of units they'll show per floor plan.
You can usually get around that on your website.
Um, there's a few ways to do it.
Um, we can actually query your website in different ways to unlock those units.
We can go look at what units are on an ILS versus on your website.
We can even look at like how's your site map availability, if you have site map or something like that, compared to your online leasing.
So most of the time hidden availability uh is not as hidden.

Uh, if you're querying the websites in the right way, we also, we do have an algorithm running in the background now as well to tell us, hey, we think there are hidden there's hidden availability here, and so like we shouldn't use that data.
But in general, yes, I think the sense is like some folks are just, they want zero exposure to any of this and so they're just like, I don't even want to use, I don't want my data in another system.
I just want, and I don't want to give you my data either.
So just let's just use the data we've got.
Usually the way they get comfortable with that is they're like, well, look, these recommendations are the same as I'd be getting for my, if you had my rent and roll data anyway.
Um, but then, like I mentioned, I think a number of our customers are going to be focused on bringing in the private rent roll data as well.
Agree.
I

It almost seems to me like.

I, I don't know.
I'm, I'm spitballing here, but I'm thinking that would be something rather than nothing.
For sure.

yeah.

So that, that, that would be important as well.
And like, like I said, I'm uh I'm willing to give you whatever, you know, visibility into my data you need to get you the most accurate, uh, forecasts.
Yeah.

you have a question?

OK.

just from my experience.

Yeah, for you, that's been fine, yeah.

Well, we, um, we're actively building out, I mean.
It's helpful to know that's your, your view on this, um.
I guess let me ask you this.
How long have you been using your arm for?
We just finished the rollout in November.
OK, so pretty new.
Mhm.
How's, how's that going?
Um, I would say for the most part, it's going pretty well.
The, when we let the system go full on without any restrictions, at, at the start, we were getting some pretty volatile pricing recommendation, you know, 5% up, 5% down day over day.
So we ended up um.
Putting some guardrails on, um, anywhere between 1 and 3% up or down per day, and 5 to 10% up or down per week depending on the property market, that kind of thing.
Uh, so it's a little more volatile.
Um, then, then I was hoping for, but again, we can get around that.
Um.
What else?
The
The, the.
lease expiration management part of it.
It's kind of, it's borrowed from LRO um which I know very, very well.
I worked with LRO for 20 years and I was hoping that LRO was gonna make some adjustments to make their lease expiration management to work a little better.

There are a couple of tweaks they could do, um, you know, specifically, you know, not lumping all the expirations together in one month to do it week over week because, you know, for example, August 5th versus August 25th can make a big difference if school starts August 15th, um, and it AIRM didn't take that ball and run with it, like I hope they would have, um.

So that's probably my, my biggest challenge with it is, is this July, August, September renewals and lease expirations, um, getting around the seasonal cause, cause some, some areas, I'm sure you guys have seen this seasonality before and after school starts, it's just, it's just the drop off is incredible and um and Operations has a tendency to freak out about that a little bit.

Um, they don't wanna see those units be vacant, you know, I think if we don't lease them before school starts, that it's gonna sit vacant until October or November and it's probably a little bit of an exaggeration in most markets.
Denver could be the exception, but um,
Yeah, I, I, I had a little more control over that.
That would be great.
Yeah.
Interesting.
Got it.
So that exploration logic, or it sounds like even just the reporting on expirations and being able to see within those months, that's what would be useful to you.
Yeah, yeah, just more weekly rather than monthly.
I'd like to see it optimized for.
You know, at the week level rather than at the month level on the lease expirations.
But beyond that, I don't, I don't really have anything um bad to say about Aaron other than it takes them 4 weeks to set up a new property.
And with LOO I could do it in 1 day, literally do it one day.
And now it's, it's 4 weeks.
And so I've been getting some negative feedback from people above me on that one.
So, um, and I didn't realize that until we had actually started the rollout.
Do they know, yeah, what's what's causing that?

So they're telling me that it takes them 3+ weeks to build out the environment to get all of the, the data in and run the, run the algorithm um a few times to get pricing to where they needed to be before they can flip the switch.
Mm.
That's what I'm told anyway.
Yeah.
Interesting that that for A it would need that amount of time, but for LRO didn't.
Oh yeah,
literally, I could, when I was with Rainmaker, I could initialize a property and set it up and make it ready for the clients use the same day, no, no exaggeration.
Yeah.
And I was gonna ask, are you, did you move from LRO to AIRM?
I, I, yeah, we did, absolutely.
And I started here with, with Carol now RMR we were on LRO and so we went through the, that.
Um, transition last year.
Got it.
So now the whole portfolio is over to AIRM.
Yes.
What do you, is there anything you miss about LRO?
Besides the quick setup time, um.
No, not really.
That's the only thing I miss, if I'm being completely honest with you.
The, the AIRM has a lot more bells and whistles.
It sounds like the recommendations are maybe a bit more volatile in there than than LRO.
Yes, on, on a, on an average basis, yeah, the, the, the recommendations are more volatile.
Um, usually with LRO we didn't see, you know, 5% or more unless someone in your market did something stupid and raised their rents by like $500.
That in and of itself could push your pricing up, and that's something I, I don't miss about LRO uh one comp in with AIRM one comp can't influence your prices that way.
Um, but So it's it's kind of give and take.
Yeah.
OK.
How about the price point?
I've heard that the price point sometimes goes up as a result of the shift from LRO to A.
Has that been?
Digestible
fuel.
How much we pay per realage per unit.
I've, I've just heard sometimes there's some, some, uh, you know, stomach pain associated with that.
So it I think we got a really good deal.
Again, I, I used to work there.
I had good relationships with a lot of people there.
Um, so ours was not bad.
Ours was, you know,
I want to say it was less than 50 cents a unit, the, the price increase, and I was expecting more than that, um, and they.
Converted all of our properties for free.
No, no implementation fees or anything like that.
So, um, that, that was a fairly uh painless part of it.
Nice.
How much appetite do you think there is, like,
One of the things we have heard consistently from the market, as you might expect, they would like more transparency in the system.
They'd like to have more obvious market data in the system.
Um, one of the things we've heard is the price felt like it got high.

Is that, you know, do you, do you get the sense there's price sensitivity and RMR around what you're spending on, like, you know, if, if there's a viable alternative that's half the price, does that matter?
you mean like our, our fees per unit to use the system?
Absolutely.
Um, you know, we, most of our properties are, uh, joint venture.
Um, ownership.
So we, we have equity partners in just about all of our assets, not all of them, but most of them.
And they are all concerned about pricing is, you know, is there any way we can cut expenses, you know, without chopping off our feet?
They're, they're pretty good about spending money when they know it's gonna make them money.
Um, but, but sure, I think that, that, you know, price sensitivity is always a factor.
Um, but I think that your, your customer base is gonna look at, OK, it's, it's saving me 25 cents a unit, but Is it gonna get me the same?
Uh, revenue increase per unit that this other system will.
So the, the, the, the
The um multi-family industry has gotten pretty smart about that, I think.
If you ask me the same question 15 years ago, I would have been like, uh, they're just looking for the lowest price.
Yeah, yeah.
How do, how do you think you would get comfortable with that and like, you know, let's say that's it's $1.50 less per unit.
How would you get comfortable with these recommendations are going to give me the same lift.
Um, I think you'd be paying us.
Per unit if I'm being completely honest with you,
uh, well you guys got a great deal then.
No, no, I, I think, I think honestly, I no kidding, I think we're about 2 bucks a unit.
With ARM,
uh, cream, yeah, it, I think we, we made it, the, the change at the right time as well.
It had just started with all of the, the court.
Um, issues that they've been having, um, and so they were excited to get, you know, a former employee who's now a, you know, a director of pricing to flip over to AIRM.
So they, they were, they were, I think we may have caught him a little bit of a desperate time, um, as well.
I think we got a really good deal, um.
But Yeah.
You know, I, it, it's a tough call.
I, I would.
I, I, the simplicity of it would be nice, um.

I, I, I'd have to see, you know, the, the drop in per unit cost versus, you know, what can we expect, um, on the revenue side, on the, on the other end, and you know, the, the only way to do that is to, is to try it, at least from my, from my perspective, um, you know, the only way to figure that out is to, is to see it in action.
What's that typically
did you test AIRM before you rolled it out fully?
We did, we did.
We, we had uh 2 test properties in Florida and we tested them for 6 months and we um did an AB comparison with uh a sister community that was very similar, very close by.
So we did that for about About 6 months before we made the the commitment to go ahead and roll the rest of the the portfolio.
What were you looking for during the test?
Well, so performance revenue bump, you know, where we gonna get?
You know, an increase in revenue versus LRO where are we gonna get easier reporting, where we gonna get easier understanding and, and more user friendly interface, um.
